question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic with a crafted TopicMessageFilter whose startTime is set to epoch 0, causing the listener to replay the entire Hashgraph topic history in an order that omits integrity checks on sequenceNumber continuity, allowing the attacker to observe and confirm gaps or reordering in the historical record without any authentication?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user supply a TopicMessageFilter with a limit of Long.MAX_VALUE to the listen() method, causing integer overflow in PollingTopicListener.poll() at the cast '(int) (filter.getLimit() - context.getCount().get())', resulting in a negative pageSize that corrupts the query window and causes messages to be skipped or duplicated in the delivered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage, TopicMessageFilter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() in CompositeTopicListener only checks topicId equality and consensusTimestamp >= startTime, with no verification of sequenceNumber ordering or runningHash integrity, to receive a stream of TopicMessage objects that appear valid but contain tampered Hashgraph history without any detection?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage, TopicMessageFilter)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a subscription where filterMessage() passes a TopicMessage whose sequenceNumber is non-monotonically increasing (e.g., sequence 5 followed by sequence 3), because filterMessage() never validates sequenceNumber ordering, causing the subscriber to silently receive a reordered Hashgraph history that misrepresents the true consensus order?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSharedListener(TopicMessageFilter)] Can an unprivileged external user subscribing via SharedPollingTopicListener exploit the fact that getSharedListener() returns the same global topicMessages Flux for all filters regardless of topicId, causing messages from unrelated topics to be delivered to the subscriber if the upstream filterMessage() check is bypassed or delayed, thereby exposing cross-topic Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll(PollingContext)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a race condition in PollingTopicListener.poll() where the AtomicReference<TopicMessage> last is read as null between two concurrent poll cycles, causing startTime to reset to filter.getStartTime() and re-delivering already-seen messages, which an attacker could use to confirm duplicate delivery of Hashgraph history entries and undermine message uniqueness guarantees?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll(PollingContext)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the startTime calculation 'last.getConsensusTimestamp() + 1' in PollingTopicListener.poll() by observing that if a TopicMessage with consensusTimestamp = Long.MAX_VALUE is stored in the database (due to upstream tampering), the +1 overflows to Long.MIN_VALUE, causing all subsequent polls to query from the beginning of time and re-deliver the entire Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: subscribe(Topic)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RedisTopicListener.subscribe() method's use of .share() on a hot publisher, where a malicious Redis message injected into the 'topic.N' channel is deserialized and broadcast to all active subscribers of that topic simultaneously, causing all subscribers to receive a tampered TopicMessage that misrepresents Hashgraph history without any per-subscriber integrity check?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getTopic(TopicMessageFilter)] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic whose numeric ID maps to a Redis channel name 'topic.N' that is also writable by other processes, and if the Redis instance is accessible to any process that can publish to that channel, cause the listener to deliver fabricated TopicMessage objects with arbitrary sequenceNumber and consensusTimestamp values, effectively injecting false Hashgraph history into all subscribers?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the onBackpressureBuffer(maxBufferSize, BufferOverflowStrategy.ERROR) in SharedTopicListener.listen() by subscribing slowly and causing the buffer to overflow, which terminates the subscriber's stream mid-history, creating a gap in the delivered Hashgraph history that the subscriber cannot distinguish from a legitimate end-of-stream?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: onNext(TopicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SharedPollingTopicListener.PollingContext.onNext() method's unconditional update of lastConsensusTimestamp to topicMessage.getConsensusTimestamp(), where if a TopicMessage with a very large consensusTimestamp is delivered first (due to out-of-order DB insertion), all subsequent polls skip all messages with smaller timestamps, permanently hiding earlier Hashgraph history from all subscribers?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: onStart(Subscription)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SharedPollingTopicListener.PollingContext.onStart() method which sets lastConsensusTimestamp to DomainUtils.now() at subscription time, meaning any TopicMessage with a consensusTimestamp before the subscription moment is never polled by the shared listener, allowing an attacker who delays the first subscription to cause all pre-subscription Hashgraph history to be silently omitted from the live stream?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: recordMetric(TopicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user cause CompositeTopicListener.recordMetric() to silently skip latency recording for messages with a future consensusTimestamp (latency <= 0 check), and by observing which messages are not recorded in the 'hiero.mirror.grpc.consensus.latency' metric, infer that tampered messages with future timestamps are being delivered, confirming Hashgraph history manipulation without triggering any alert?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user open an unbounded number of concurrent gRPC subscriptions via listen(), each creating a new PollingContext and a new polling Flux on Schedulers.boundedElastic(), exhausting the bounded thread pool and causing legitimate subscribers to experience delayed or dropped message delivery, effectively denying accurate Hashgraph history to other users?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getSharedListener(TopicMessageFilter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the computeIfAbsent race condition in RedisTopicListener.getSharedListener() where two concurrent subscribers for the same topic may both call subscribe(topic) before the first result is stored, creating two independent Redis subscriptions for the same channel and causing duplicate message delivery that makes the Hashgraph history appear to contain repeated entries?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: unsubscribe(Topic)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger RedisTopicListener.unsubscribe() by cancelling their subscription, which removes the shared Flux from topicMessages map, causing all other active subscribers on the same topic to lose their shared listener and miss messages published during the re-subscription window, creating a gap in the Hashgraph history delivered to those subscribers?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of runningHash verification in the entire TopicListener pipeline — where TopicMessage.runningHash is stored but never validated against the previous message's hash — to receive a stream of messages where any message's content has been altered without detection, undermining the cryptographic integrity of Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe with a TopicMessageFilter whose endTime is set to a value just 1 nanosecond after startTime, causing the listener to deliver only a single message window, and by iterating this across many subscriptions, enumerate the entire Hashgraph history one message at a time to reconstruct a complete tampered timeline without triggering rate limits?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll(PollingContext)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the PollingTopicListener.poll() method's use of 'Math.min(limit, listenerProperties.getMaxPageSize())' where maxPageSize defaults to 5000, causing the listener to deliver up to 5000 messages per poll cycle without any per-message integrity validation, allowing bulk retrieval of potentially tampered Hashgraph history entries in a single poll?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the METRIC constant 'hiero_mirror_grpc_listener' and METRIC_TAG 'mode' are exposed as observable metrics, allowing the attacker to monitor listener mode (POLL/REDIS/SHARED_POLL) and timing patterns to infer when the Hashgraph history database is being queried and time injection attacks accordingly?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll(PollingContext)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the jitter(0.1) applied to the RepeatSpec in PollingTopicListener.listen() to cause non-deterministic polling intervals, creating timing windows where messages inserted into the database between polls are missed, resulting in gaps in the delivered Hashgraph history that the subscriber cannot detect?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the delaySubscription(interval, scheduler) in PollingTopicListener.listen() to create a predictable window before the first poll, during which messages inserted into the database are not yet delivered, and by timing a subscription precisely, cause the subscriber to miss the first batch of Hashgraph history messages?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getTopicListener()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the switch-case in CompositeTopicListener.getTopicListener() where the default case throws UnsupportedOperationException, by observing error responses to infer the current listener type configuration, and then craft attacks specific to the active listener type (POLL/REDIS/SHARED_POLL) to manipulate Hashgraph history delivery?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the publishOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic(), false, listenerProperties.getPrefetch()) in SharedTopicListener.listen() where prefetch defaults to 48, causing up to 48 messages to be pre-fetched and buffered per subscriber, and if the underlying data changes between prefetch and delivery, the subscriber receives stale or inconsistent Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/TopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll(PollingContext)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the findByFilter() query in PollingTopicListener.poll() which orders results by consensusTimestamp ascending, where if two TopicMessages have identical consensusTimestamps (a condition not prevented by the listener), the ordering becomes non-deterministic and the delivered Hashgraph history may differ between subscribers, allowing an attacker to confirm timestamp collision vulnerabilities?
